titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Lady Lansdowne, August 14, 1816
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Texas A & M University, University of Tennessee, Wake Forest University, Xavier University of Louisiana, Southern Methodist University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date: June 25, 2026

respStmt.orgName: Bibliothèques municipales de la Ville de Genève

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of the Bibliothèques municipales de la Ville de Genève
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Lady Lansdowne,

sourceDesc.bibl: In addition to our edited version, this letter was previously published in

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

div.p:
My dear Lady Lansdowne
Can you think of me when
you are so far away? - Yes
I must believe that you
can since you were so incre
diblyincredibly kind as to write to
me the very day before you
left England - I assure you
that if you had not written
I never should have sus-
pectedsuspected you of neglecting
either my father or myself
so firm is my opinion of
the constancy of your re-
gardregard for those whom you
have once honored with
any portion of your great esteem.

I regret that letter of yours
which is lying at the dead
letter office at Canterbury
119
I rather think that one of
mine has not reached you
but I cannot recollect any
thing it contained except
a translation of some French
lines of Arnauds on a leaf
which my father sent.

This note probably will
find you at Geneva. I hope
you will have the goodness
to remember us affectionate
=lyaffectionately to our friends there -
especially to excellent M.
Dumont . - tho he has been
very naughty about writing
- not one letter have we
received from him since
he went to Geneva - Is he so
taken up in making new consti
-tutionsconstitutions that he cannot write
a letter to his old friends ? -

- But I have no right to reproach
my friends much for not
writing as I am so very lazy
myself - I enclose a notice
which I wrote for as a tribute
of regard to my friend Mrs.
Elizabeth Hamilton whose
death probably you have
seen in the papers - I remem
berremember 10 or 12 years ago M.
Pictet gave a very good ac
countaccount in his journal Bri-
-tanniqueBritannique of Mrs. Hamilton's
letters on Education - Per-
haps>Perhaps he may now like
to see this notice & I hope he
will take some means
of doing justice to her me
-morymemory either in the French
papers or journals - Does
Is the Journal Britannique
still continued ? -

I hope my dear Lady
Lansdowne
you will do
me the favor to write and
tell me the how you like
Switzerland - & Italy -
I hope you will not see all
the churches & palaces which
120
Eustace describes - Half of them
I think would be quite enough
for me - That book gave me
an indigestion a surfeit of
churches & palaces - And I
thought I never should have
got alive out of the Vatican
I was so tired of it - Nevertheless
I acknowledge that many parts
of the book are very eloquent -
- But there is too much of every
thing - & especially too much fine
writing -

I think Lord & Lady Byron's af-
fairaffair was all settled before
you left England & that he
had gone off with the univer
saluniversal execration of England.

- Perhaps you will meet
him again in SwisserlandSwitzerland
I hear he is now at Geneva

- How Madame de Stael & he
will talk - I wish I could hear
them provided I could be
now & then relieved & refresh
ed with a little common-
sense from you & Lord Lans-
downe - Lady Caroline Lamb
has just published a book in
which there is not one grain
of common sense & no pretine
- something a little unintelligible about aberra-
-tionsaberrations & radiance & gas & sparks & corrosive
air oxydating the brain - but if men of science
will not insist upon exactness or sense it will
pass well enough - As Mr. Bentham wd. say it
is good enough for poetry - Do not my dear
Lady L give any copy of the lines because
I promised that you shd not - The lines are
not written by any of my family I assure
you upon my word & I trust you believe I wd
not put my word to a falsehood. They were
written by a young gentleman whom I never
saw & whose name I have not been able
yet to learn - I have so good an opinion of
Davy's head & heart that I think it wd. do him
service to read them - but I shd. not like to
be the person to read them to him - shd you?

Things of this sort have such a different
degree of interest on the spot & at a distance
that I almost repent having written them
for you. Heaven knows where you may be
or what grand mountain, church or
person your head may be full of when this
comes to your hand! - Pray write & tell me
& mention your children - accounts of
children are always most interesting to us

If ever your children are in want of amuse
ment - But I will not tell you that now - be-
cause they cannot be in want of amusement
travelling - & with you.

All the young people of this family remember
you with pleasure as lively as if they had seen
you but yesterday - There is one walk you went
with us which has ever since been called the Lansdowne
walk - & a stool you sat on in my room goes by
the name of the Lansdowne stool - your dear
first happy home after your marriage hangs over
the chimney place in the room we always sat
in & many, many, many times it is looked
at by every individual in this house. - constant
ly with feelings of gratitude - & pleasure -
and hope that we shall sometime see
you again -

Mrs. Edgeworth & my father present their
affectionate respects to you & to Lord Lansdowne

I rather flatter myself that he does not
quite forget me - If he could know with what
delight my father in the midst of his worst
illness last year Session read Lord L's speeches
in parliament I am sure it wd. gratify him

Pray write to me & convince me
that my letters are not troublesome
to you abroad
121
div.closer:
Your Lady
affectionate &
sincere
Maria Edgeworth
